topic: Energy transfer by melting, boiling, evaporation, conduction, and convection

questions.question_summary: Analyzes a temperature-time graph for two different solid substances to compare their melting points during a heating process.
questions.reasoning_focus: Interpreting horizontal sections of a heating curve as phase transition points (melting).

questions.question_summary: Describes the process of evaporation as the mechanism where more-energetic molecules escape from the surface of a liquid.
questions.reasoning_focus: Identifying the energy-based particle behavior that causes cooling and state change at a liquid surface.

questions.question_summary: Evaluates an experiment using wax and pins on rods to compare the rate of thermal conduction across different materials.
questions.reasoning_focus: Determining the relative conductivity of materials based on the speed of thermal energy transfer.

questions.question_summary: Requires distinguishing between specific heat capacity (raising temperature) and the energy required to melt a unit mass.
questions.reasoning_focus: Defining the physical significance of thermal constants related to temperature change vs. phase change.

questions.question_summary: Identifies the specific particle types (individual molecules) that escape from a water surface during the evaporation process.
questions.reasoning_focus: Understanding the scale and type of particles involved in liquid-to-gas phase transitions.

questions.question_summary: Uses a practical example of copper and wooden rods in ice to explain how different conduction rates affect temperature perception.
questions.reasoning_focus: Relating the feel of a material to its ability to transfer thermal energy away from or to an object.

questions.question_summary: Prompts a description of an experimental setup to compare the thermal conductivity of various metals and glass.
questions.reasoning_focus: Designing a fair test to measure heat transfer rates through solids.

questions.question_summary: Asks for the identification of convection as a heat transfer method and an explanation of how convection currents warm the air in a room.
questions.reasoning_focus: Explaining how changes in air density lead to the circulation of thermal energy.

questions.question_summary: Requires suggestions for minimizing thermal energy loss from a beaker during a cooling investigation.
questions.reasoning_focus: Applying knowledge of conduction and evaporation to practical laboratory scenarios to reduce heat loss.
